articles.headline: India Unveils Ambitious 'National Green Hydrogen Corridor' Project
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government today launched the 'National Green Hydrogen Corridor' project, a major step towards achieving India's ambitious climate targets and energy independence.
• The project aims to establish dedicated infrastructure for the production, storage, and transportation of green hydrogen across key industrial hubs.
• It includes setting up large-scale electrolyser manufacturing facilities, pipelines for hydrogen transport, and storage depots.
• The initiative is a cornerstone of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission, targeting a production capacity of 5 million tonnes per annum by 2030.
• This move is expected to attract significant domestic and foreign investment, creating numerous job opportunities in the green energy sector.
gsSubTopics: Energy
Infrastructure
Environment Conservation & Pollution Control
Science & Technology-developments & their applications & effects in everyday life
Economic Development
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) launched in 2023.
Target: 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen production by 2030.
Corridor aims to connect production sites to consumption centres.
Focus on reducing fossil fuel imports and achieving net-zero emissions by 2070.
Scheme components include incentives for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategic rationale behind prioritizing green hydrogen for energy security and climate goals.
Analyze the economic implications of the Green Hydrogen Corridor, including job creation, investment, and export potential.
Examine the technological challenges and opportunities in scaling up green hydrogen production and infrastructure.
Evaluate the role of government policies and international collaborations in realizing the vision of a hydrogen economy.
Critically assess the environmental benefits and potential drawbacks of large-scale green hydrogen deployment.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration showing a map of India with proposed Green Hydrogen Corridor routes and production facilities.
articles.trivia: India's target of 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen by 2030 is equivalent to displacing about 50 million tonnes of fossil fuels.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Inflationary Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) today decided to maintain the repo rate at its current level for the seventh consecutive time.
• This decision comes amidst persistent global inflationary pressures and the need to support domestic economic growth.
• RBI Governor highlighted the robust growth trajectory of the Indian economy but cautioned about potential headwinds from global commodity prices.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting growth.
• Experts anticipate continued vigilance from the RBI, with future policy moves contingent on evolving inflation and growth dynamics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
prelimsPoints: Repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
MPC is a six-member body responsible for setting benchmark interest rates.
Primary objective of monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Inflation target for RBI is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth.
Discuss the impact of a 'status quo' monetary policy on various sectors of the Indian economy.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the MPC framework in achieving its stated objectives.
Examine the role of global economic factors in shaping India's domestic monetary policy decisions.
articles.imageDescription: RBI headquarters building in Mumbai.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) was constituted in 2016.

articles.headline: ISRO's 'Shakti' Mission Successfully Deploys Advanced Earth Observation Satellite
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched the 'Shakti' mission, deploying an advanced Earth observation satellite into orbit.
• The satellite is equipped with cutting-edge hyperspectral imaging capabilities, designed for detailed monitoring of natural resources and environmental changes.
• Data from 'Shakti' will be crucial for agricultural planning, disaster management, urban development, and climate studies.
• This mission reinforces India's growing prowess in space technology and its commitment to utilizing space for societal benefit.
• The satellite has a planned operational life of five years, providing continuous high-resolution data.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Space Technology
Disaster Management
prelimsPoints: ISRO is India's national space agency.
Hyperspectral imaging allows for detailed analysis of materials on Earth's surface.
Earth observation satellites are vital for applications like weather forecasting, climate monitoring, and resource mapping.
PSLV and GSLV are ISRO's primary launch vehicles.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Earth observation satellites for India's development and security.
Analyze how hyperspectral imaging technology can contribute to sustainable agriculture and environmental monitoring.
Examine the challenges and future prospects for India's space program in the context of global competition.
Evaluate the socio-economic benefits derived from ISRO's various satellite missions.
articles.imageDescription: Artist's impression of the 'Shakti' Earth observation satellite in orbit.
articles.trivia: India is one of the few countries with indigenous capability to launch satellites and develop advanced remote sensing technologies.

articles.headline: Parliamentary Standing Committee Recommends Reforms for Electoral Bonds Scheme
articles.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee on Law and Justice today tabled its report recommending significant reforms to the existing Electoral Bonds Scheme.
• The committee emphasized the need for greater transparency and accountability in political funding to strengthen democratic processes.
• Key recommendations include lowering the minimum denomination of bonds and introducing a mechanism for public disclosure of donor details.
• The report also suggested exploring alternative methods of political financing that are more equitable and transparent.
• This comes amidst ongoing debates and public scrutiny regarding the opaque nature of the current electoral bonds system.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Accountability and transparency in governance
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bonds scheme introduced in 2017 via Finance Act.
Allows citizens/corporate bodies to donate to political parties anonymously.
Bonds issued by State Bank of India (SBI) in specific denominations.
Valid for 15 days from the date of issue.
Only political parties registered under Section 29A of RPA and securing at least 1% votes in last general/assembly election are eligible to receive bonds.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the arguments for and against the Electoral Bonds Scheme.
Discuss how lack of transparency in political funding impacts democratic accountability and fairness of elections.
Examine the various recommendations for electoral reforms concerning political financing in India.
Evaluate the role of parliamentary committees in scrutinizing government policies and suggesting improvements.
articles.imageDescription: Image of the Parliament building in New Delhi.
articles.trivia: The Supreme Court is currently hearing petitions challenging the constitutionality of the Electoral Bonds Scheme.

articles.headline: India-ASEAN Summit Concludes with Focus on Maritime Security and Trade Enhancement
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Summit concluded today, with leaders emphasizing deeper cooperation in maritime security, trade, and digital connectivity.
• Discussions focused on countering non-traditional security threats in the Indo-Pacific, including piracy, terrorism, and illegal fishing.
• A new roadmap for enhancing bilateral trade to $300 billion by 2030 was adopted, alongside initiatives for supply chain resilience.
• India reiterated its commitment to ASEAN centrality in its 'Act East' policy and the broader Indo-Pacific vision.
• Agreements were also signed on cultural exchange programs and capacity building in emerging technologies.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
keyTerms: SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region)
prelimsPoints: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) founded in 1967.
Members: Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Brunei, Vietnam, Laos, Myanmar, Cambodia.
India became a Sectoral Dialogue Partner of ASEAN in 1992, Dialogue Partner in 1995, and Summit-level partner in 2002.
India's 'Act East Policy' is a cornerstone of its engagement with ASEAN countries.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of India's 'Act East Policy' in the context of its strategic engagement with ASEAN.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for enhancing maritime security cooperation between India and ASEAN in the Indo-Pacific region.
Evaluate the potential for increasing bilateral trade and investment between India and ASEAN, identifying key sectors for growth.
Examine how India's role in ASEAN contributes to regional stability and economic integration.
articles.imageDescription: Leaders from India and ASEAN member states at a summit meeting.
articles.trivia: The theme of this year's summit revolved around 'Strengthening Partnership for a Sustainable and Resilient Future'.

articles.headline: New Tiger Census Shows Promising Recovery in Western Ghats Landscape
articles.explainer:
• The latest All India Tiger Estimation (AITE) report indicates a significant recovery in tiger populations across the Western Ghats landscape.
• Conservation efforts, including habitat improvement, anti-poaching measures, and community involvement, are credited for the positive trend.
• The report highlights specific areas within the Western Ghats, such as the Periyar and Anamalai Tiger Reserves, showing robust growth.
• This news underscores the success of India's Project Tiger and its commitment to biodiversity conservation.
• Challenges remain in ensuring habitat connectivity and mitigating human-wildlife conflict in the region.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity
Wildlife Protection
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: All India Tiger Estimation (AITE) is conducted every four years.
India is home to over 70% of the world's tiger population.
Project Tiger launched in 1973.
Western Ghats is one of the 36 global biodiversity hotspots.
NTCA is the statutory body under MoEFCC for tiger conservation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the factors contributing to the success of tiger conservation in India, with specific reference to the Western Ghats.
Analyze the socio-economic implications of tiger conservation for local communities and indigenous populations.
Examine the challenges in maintaining habitat connectivity and managing human-wildlife conflict in densely populated areas.
Evaluate the role of international cooperation and technological advancements (e.g., M-STrIPES) in wildlife protection.
articles.imageDescription: A majestic Bengal Tiger captured in its natural habitat in the Western Ghats.
articles.trivia: The Western Ghats is older than the Himalayas and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

articles.headline: NEP 2020: Report Highlights Implementation Challenges and Path Forward
articles.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by a government-appointed task force today highlighted both successes and persistent challenges in the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• While significant progress has been made in curriculum reforms and digital learning initiatives, issues like teacher training and infrastructure gaps remain.
• The report emphasized the need for increased public spending on education and greater collaboration between central and state governments.
• Recommendations include targeted capacity building programs for educators and phased implementation strategies for complex reforms.
• The policy aims to transform India's education system from school to higher education over the next decade.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
Aims for 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in preschool to secondary level by 2030.
Proposes 5+3+3+4 curricular structure.
Focus on multidisciplinary education, vocational training, and critical thinking.
Recommends increasing public investment in education to 6% of GDP.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of NEP 2020 in addressing the shortcomings of India's education system.
Discuss the major challenges in implementing NEP 2020, particularly concerning teacher readiness, infrastructure, and funding.
Suggest concrete measures to overcome the implementation hurdles and ensure equitable access to quality education.
Evaluate the role of technology and digital platforms in achieving the objectives of NEP 2020.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing educational advancement.
articles.trivia: The NEP 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India.

articles.headline: Major Boost to 'Make in India' in Defense: New Drone Manufacturing Facility Inaugurated
articles.explainer:
• In a significant push for indigenous defense manufacturing, a state-of-the-art Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) manufacturing facility was inaugurated in Bengaluru today.
• The facility, a joint venture between an Indian private firm and a foreign defense major, will produce advanced drones for military and civilian applications.
• This initiative aligns with the 'Make in India' and 'Aatmanirbhar Bharat' campaigns, aiming to reduce defense import dependency.
• The plant is expected to create thousands of skilled jobs and foster a robust defense industrial ecosystem.
• The drones will incorporate cutting-edge surveillance, reconnaissance, and combat capabilities.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Defence Technology
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: Bengaluru is a major hub for aerospace and defense industries in India.
'Make in India' initiative launched in 2014 to promote domestic manufacturing.
'Aatmanirbhar Bharat' (Self-Reliant India) emphasizes self-reliance across sectors, including defense.
Drones are increasingly used for surveillance, logistics, and combat in modern warfare.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of indigenous defense manufacturing for India's national security and economic growth.
Analyze the role of private sector participation and foreign collaborations in boosting India's defense industrial base.
Examine the ethical and security challenges associated with the proliferation and use of advanced drone technology.
Evaluate the impact of 'Make in India' in defense on reducing import dependency and creating a skilled workforce.
articles.imageDescription: Advanced military drones on display at a manufacturing facility.
articles.trivia: India aims to achieve a defense manufacturing turnover of $25 billion by 2025, including $5 billion in exports.

articles.headline: High Court Directs State to Implement Comprehensive Urban Waste Management Plan
articles.explainer:
• The State High Court today issued a landmark directive ordering the state government to formulate and implement a comprehensive urban waste management plan within six months.
• The ruling came in response to a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) highlighting severe environmental degradation and public health hazards caused by unscientific waste disposal.
• The court emphasized the 'polluter pays' principle and the need for decentralized waste processing units.
• It also mandated strict enforcement of solid waste management rules and public awareness campaigns.
• This judgment sets a precedent for other states grappling with similar waste management challenges.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Judiciary
prelimsPoints: Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016, replaced the Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000.
PIL allows public-spirited individuals or organizations to approach courts for public good.
Polluter Pays Principle states that those who produce pollution should bear the costs of managing it.
Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) are primarily responsible for waste management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-faceted challenges faced by Indian cities in effective urban waste management.
Analyze the role of judicial activism, particularly through PILs, in compelling government action on environmental issues.
Suggest innovative solutions and best practices for sustainable solid waste management in urban areas.
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing environmental laws and policies in addressing waste pollution.
articles.imageDescription: An illustration of proper waste segregation and recycling bins in an urban setting.
articles.trivia: India generates approximately 1.5 lakh metric tonnes of municipal solid waste per day.

articles.headline: Indian Scientists Develop Novel Drug for Neglected Tropical Disease
articles.explainer:
• A team of Indian scientists at the National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research (NIPER) has announced the development of a novel drug candidate for a prevalent neglected tropical disease (NTD).
• The breakthrough offers a more affordable and effective treatment option, potentially revolutionizing NTD management in resource-limited settings.
• Pre-clinical trials have shown promising results, with human trials expected to commence within the next year.
• This achievement underscores India's growing capabilities in pharmaceutical research and development, particularly for diseases affecting vulnerable populations.
• The government has pledged support for expediting the drug's clinical development and accessibility.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Health
Biotechnology
prelimsPoints: Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) are a diverse group of communicable diseases prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions.
Examples include Dengue, Chikungunya, Kala-azar, Lymphatic Filariasis, Leprosy.
NIPERs are national institutes for pharmaceutical education and research.
India has a significant burden of NTDs and is a major producer of generic drugs.
mainsPoints: Discuss the socio-economic burden of Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) in India and globally.
Analyze the role of indigenous research and development in addressing public health challenges, particularly for affordable healthcare solutions.
Examine the policy measures required to accelerate drug discovery and ensure equitable access to new treatments for NTDs.
Evaluate the challenges faced by pharmaceutical research in developing countries and how they can be overcome.
articles.imageDescription: Scientists working in a laboratory setting, symbolizing medical research.
articles.trivia: India launched the National Action Plan for Elimination of Kala-azar (NAPEK) to eradicate the disease by 2023.

articles.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Enter Critical Phase
articles.explainer:
• Negotiations for the India-European Union Free Trade Agreement (FTA) have entered a critical and final phase, with both sides expressing optimism for a breakthrough.
• Senior negotiators met today to resolve outstanding issues related to tariffs, intellectual property rights, and market access for services.
• A successful FTA is expected to significantly boost bilateral trade and investment, creating new opportunities for businesses and consumers.
• Key areas of contention include agricultural subsidies and data localization norms, requiring careful diplomatic efforts.
• The agreement aims to unlock the full potential of the India-EU strategic partnership.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: European Union (EU) is a political and economic union of 27 member states.
India-EU relations elevated to a 'Strategic Partnership' in 2007.
FTA negotiations began in 2007, paused in 2013, and resumed in 2022.
The EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential benefits and challenges of a comprehensive Free Trade Agreement between India and the European Union.
Discuss the key contentious issues in the India-EU FTA negotiations and their implications for India's domestic industries.
Evaluate how a successful FTA with the EU could strengthen India's position in global trade and supply chains.
Examine the broader geopolitical significance of enhanced India-EU economic cooperation.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union waving side-by-side.
articles.trivia: The EU is India's third-largest trading partner, accounting for over 10% of India's total trade.

articles.headline: India on Track to Achieve 500 GW Renewable Energy Target by 2030
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy today announced that India is well on its way to achieving its ambitious target of 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
• Recent policy initiatives, increased private sector investment, and technological advancements have accelerated the deployment of solar and wind power projects.
• The report highlighted a rapid increase in installed renewable energy capacity, surpassing initial projections.
• This achievement is crucial for India's energy security, carbon emission reduction, and global climate commitments.
• Challenges related to grid integration, storage solutions, and land acquisition are being actively addressed.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy
Economic Development
prelimsPoints: India pledged 500 GW non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030 at COP26 (Glasgow).
India aims for net-zero emissions by 2070.
Solar and wind power are the largest contributors to India's renewable energy mix.
Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal agency.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of India's 500 GW renewable energy target for its climate commitments and energy security.
Discuss the key drivers and policy frameworks that have facilitated the rapid expansion of renewable energy in India.
Examine the challenges associated with large-scale renewable energy integration into the national grid and potential solutions.
Evaluate the economic benefits and job creation potential of India's renewable energy transition.
articles.imageDescription: Vast solar panel farms stretching into the horizon, symbolizing renewable energy growth.
articles.trivia: India is the third-largest energy consuming country in the world.

articles.headline: Ancient Buddhist Site in Ladakh Proposed for UNESCO World Heritage Status
articles.explainer:
• The Indian government has formally proposed an ancient Buddhist monastery complex in Ladakh for inclusion in the UNESCO World Heritage List.
• The site, known for its unique monastic architecture, pristine murals, and invaluable Buddhist scriptures, represents a significant cultural and historical legacy.
• This nomination aims to protect and preserve the fragile heritage of the Himalayan region and promote cultural tourism.
• UNESCO experts are expected to conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the site's outstanding universal value.
• Inclusion would bring international recognition and support for conservation efforts.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: UNESCO World Heritage Sites are places of outstanding universal value to humanity.
Ladakh is known for its unique Tibetan Buddhist culture.
ASI is the premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India.
India has 42 UNESCO World Heritage Sites (as of 2023 end).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Buddhist heritage sites in India for understanding its cultural and historical linkages with other Asian countries.
Analyze the challenges in preserving ancient monastic complexes in geographically remote and climatically harsh regions like Ladakh.
Evaluate the benefits and responsibilities associated with a site being designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Examine the role of cultural tourism in promoting local economies while ensuring sustainable heritage conservation.
articles.imageDescription: An ancient Buddhist monastery nestled amidst the mountains of Ladakh.
articles.trivia: The Hemis Monastery in Ladakh is one of the largest and wealthiest monasteries in the region.

articles.headline: Government Launches 'Agri-Tech Innovation Fund' for Sustainable Farming
articles.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare today launched the 'Agri-Tech Innovation Fund', a new initiative to promote sustainable and climate-resilient agriculture.
• The fund will provide financial support and mentorship to startups and researchers developing innovative solutions for the agricultural sector.
• Focus areas include precision agriculture, water management, crop diversification, and post-harvest technology.
• The initiative aims to enhance farmers' income, reduce agricultural waste, and improve food security through technological interventions.
• This move is part of the government's broader strategy to modernize Indian agriculture and address challenges posed by climate change.
gsSubTopics: Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Government schemes like PM-KISAN, Fasal Bima Yojana, e-NAM aim to support farmers.
Agri-Tech startups leverage technology to improve agricultural productivity and efficiency.
Climate change poses significant threats to agricultural output in India.
Focus on 'per drop more crop' for water management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical role of technological innovation in addressing the challenges faced by Indian agriculture, including climate change and low productivity.
Analyze how initiatives like the 'Agri-Tech Innovation Fund' can promote entrepreneurship and attract investment in the agricultural sector.
Examine the potential impact of precision agriculture and post-harvest technologies on enhancing farmers' income and reducing food waste.
Evaluate the government's strategy for modernizing agriculture and ensuring food security in the long run.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer using a tablet to monitor crops, symbolizing agri-tech integration.
articles.trivia: Agriculture is the largest employer in India, employing over 50% of the workforce.

articles.headline: National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0 Unveiled to Counter Evolving Threats
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) today unveiled the 'National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0', designed to bolster India's defenses against increasingly sophisticated cyber threats.
• The updated strategy emphasizes a multi-stakeholder approach, involving government, private sector, academia, and international partners.
• Key pillars include robust critical infrastructure protection, enhanced cyber deterrence capabilities, and a focus on skill development in cybersecurity.
• It also addresses challenges posed by emerging technologies like AI and quantum computing, and the need for proactive threat intelligence.
• The strategy aims to create a secure and resilient cyberspace for India's digital economy and national security.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: India's first National Cyber Security Policy was launched in 2013.
CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) is the nodal agency for cybersecurity incidents.
Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) includes sectors like energy, finance, transport, and telecommunications.
Cyber warfare involves the use of cyberattacks for military or political objectives.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving landscape of cyber threats and their implications for India's national security and digital economy.
Discuss the key features and strategic objectives of the National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0.
Examine the challenges in implementing a comprehensive cyber security framework, including skill gaps, international cooperation, and legal aspects.
Evaluate the role of emerging technologies like AI and quantum computing in both enhancing and threatening cybersecurity.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract digital network lines representing cybersecurity and data protection.
articles.trivia: India ranks among the top countries most targeted by cyberattacks globally.

articles.headline: Digital India Initiative Expands with New Unified Citizen Services Portal
articles.explainer:
• Building on the success of the Digital India program, the government today launched a new unified citizen services portal, integrating multiple government services under a single digital platform.
• The portal aims to enhance ease of access to public services, reduce red tape, and improve transparency and efficiency in governance.
• Citizens can now access services ranging from birth certificates and land records to utility bill payments and grievance redressal through one interface.
• This initiative leverages advanced technologies like AI-powered chatbots and blockchain for secure data management.
• The expansion is expected to further bridge the digital divide and empower citizens with seamless access to government services.
gsSubTopics: E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: Digital India program launched in 2015.
Aims to transform India into a digitally empowered society and knowledge economy.
Key pillars include Digital Infrastructure, Governance & Services on Demand, and Digital Empowerment of Citizens.
E-governance focuses on using ICT to improve government services.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and impact of the Digital India program in enhancing public service delivery and citizen engagement.
Analyze the benefits of a unified citizen services portal in improving governance, transparency, and reducing corruption.
Examine the challenges associated with digital inclusion, data security, and ensuring equitable access to e-governance services.
Evaluate how technology can be leveraged to further streamline government processes and foster participatory governance.
articles.imageDescription: A person using a smartphone to access online government services.
articles.trivia: India's JAM (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile) trinity has been instrumental in direct benefit transfers and financial inclusion.

articles.headline: G20 Finance Ministers Meet to Discuss Global Economic Cooperation and Debt Relief
articles.explainer:
• Finance Ministers and Central Bank Governors from G20 nations convened today to address pressing global economic challenges, including inflation, growth slowdown, and debt vulnerabilities.
• Discussions focused on coordinating macroeconomic policies, strengthening financial stability, and promoting sustainable development.
• A key agenda item was the implementation of debt relief initiatives for low-income countries and reforms to multilateral development banks.
• India, as a prominent G20 member, reiterated its call for equitable global growth and climate financing for developing nations.
• The meeting aimed to build consensus on strategies to foster a resilient and inclusive global economy.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: G20 (Group of Twenty) is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
It works to address major issues related to the global economy.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023.
Common Framework for Debt Treatments beyond the DSSI (Debt Service Suspension Initiative) is a G20 initiative.
mainsPoints: Analyze the role of the G20 in fostering global economic stability and addressing contemporary challenges like inflation and debt crises.
Discuss India's priorities and contributions to the G20 agenda, particularly concerning climate finance and equitable growth.
Examine the effectiveness of multilateral platforms like the G20 in coordinating international responses to economic downturns.
Evaluate the need for reforms in multilateral development banks to better serve the needs of developing countries.
articles.imageDescription: G20 logo prominently displayed at an international conference.
articles.trivia: The G20 represents around two-thirds of the world's population, 85% of global GDP, and 75% of global trade.

articles.headline: India-Pakistan Hold Dialogue on Indus Waters Treaty Review Amidst Climate Concerns
articles.explainer:
• Officials from India and Pakistan held bilateral talks today to review aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), focusing on current water-sharing arrangements and climate change impacts.
• The dialogue comes amidst increasing concerns over water scarcity and the changing hydrological patterns in the Indus basin due to global warming.
• Both sides discussed proposals for technical adjustments to the treaty and mechanisms for dispute resolution.
• While the IWT has largely withstood conflicts, climate change presents new challenges requiring cooperative solutions.
• The talks aim to ensure equitable and sustainable water management for both riparian states.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) signed in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
Allocates Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral commission for implementation of the treaty.
The treaty is considered one of the most successful water-sharing agreements globally.
mainsPoints: Analyze the enduring significance of the Indus Waters Treaty as a framework for water sharing between India and Pakistan.
Discuss how climate change and increasing water scarcity are challenging the existing provisions of the IWT.
Examine the potential for India and Pakistan to enhance cooperation on transboundary water management and disaster preparedness.
Evaluate the role of water diplomacy in mitigating tensions and fostering regional stability in South Asia.
articles.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River basin and its tributaries flowing through India and Pakistan.
articles.trivia: The Indus River system is one of the largest river systems in the world, originating in the Tibetan Plateau.

articles.headline: Government Announces New Policy to Boost Critical Minerals Exploration and Mining
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government today unveiled a new policy aimed at accelerating the exploration and mining of critical minerals essential for India's energy transition and high-tech manufacturing sectors.
• The policy introduces incentives for private sector participation, advanced exploration technologies, and streamlined regulatory clearances.
• Focus minerals include Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel, and Rare Earth Elements, crucial for EV batteries, solar panels, and defense applications.
• This strategic move seeks to reduce India's reliance on imports for these vital resources and enhance supply chain security.
• The policy also emphasizes sustainable mining practices and community engagement in mineral-rich regions.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Critical minerals are essential for economic development and national security.
India is largely import-dependent for many critical minerals.
Lithium is key for Electric Vehicle (EV) batteries.
Rare Earth Elements are used in high-tech electronics and defense applications.
Geological Survey of India (GSI) is the primary agency for mineral exploration.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of critical minerals for India's economic growth, energy transition, and national security.
Discuss the key provisions of the new mining policy and its potential impact on domestic exploration and production of critical minerals.
Examine the challenges associated with critical mineral mining, including environmental concerns, technological gaps, and geopolitical dependencies.
Evaluate India's efforts to secure critical mineral supply chains through domestic exploration and international partnerships.
articles.imageDescription: Various critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, etc.) displayed with technological devices.
articles.trivia: India recently discovered significant lithium reserves in Jammu & Kashmir.

articles.headline: Gender Budgeting Report Highlights Progress and Gaps in Women-Centric Schemes
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Finance today released its annual Gender Budgeting Statement, reporting on the allocation and expenditure for women-centric schemes and programs.
• The report highlighted increased allocation in key sectors like education, health, and skill development, showing progress towards gender equality.
• However, it also identified implementation gaps and the need for more targeted interventions to address specific challenges faced by women.
• Recommendations include strengthening monitoring mechanisms and ensuring gender-disaggregated data collection for better policy formulation.
• Gender budgeting is a tool to analyze the government's budget from a gender perspective, ensuring equitable resource distribution.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Gender Budgeting is not a separate budget but an analysis of the government budget from a gender perspective.
It was introduced in India in 2005-06.
It comprises two parts: Part A (100% women-specific schemes) and Part B (schemes with at least 30% allocation for women).
SDG 5 aims to achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept and objectives of gender budgeting in India, and its role in promoting women's empowerment.
Analyze the progress and persistent challenges highlighted in the latest Gender Budgeting Statement regarding women-centric schemes.
Examine the importance of gender-disaggregated data and robust monitoring mechanisms for effective policy implementation.
Evaluate the impact of government schemes on various aspects of women's lives, including economic, social, and political empowerment.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic showing gender budgeting allocations across different sectors.
articles.trivia: India was one of the first countries in Asia to adopt gender budgeting.

highlights: India Launches Ambitious 'National Green Hydrogen Corridor' Project to boost energy independence and climate goals.
RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Inflationary Concerns, signaling cautious approach to economic stability.
ISRO's 'Shakti' Mission Successfully Deploys Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for critical resource monitoring.
India-ASEAN Summit Concludes with Focus on Maritime Security and Trade Enhancement, strengthening regional ties.
National Cyber Security Strategy 2.0 Unveiled to Counter Evolving Threats, aiming for a secure digital India.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the 'National Green Hydrogen Mission', consider the following statements:
1. The mission aims to achieve a green hydrogen production capacity of 5 million tonnes per annum by 2030.
2. It primarily focuses on reducing India's reliance on renewable energy sources.
3. Incentives for electrolyser manufacturing are a key component of the mission.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) targets a production capacity of 5 million tonnes per annum by 2030. Statement 2 is incorrect. The mission aims to reduce reliance on *fossil fuels*, not renewable energy sources, by promoting green hydrogen derived from renewables. Statement 3 is correct. Incentives for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production are key components to boost the sector.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for setting the benchmark interest rates in India?
options: (a) Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Board of Directors
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body in India, constituted in 2016, that is responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate (repo rate) to achieve the inflation target.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding Earth Observation Satellites:
1. They are primarily used for weather forecasting and climate monitoring.
2. Hyperspectral imaging capabilities allow for detailed analysis of materials on Earth's surface.
3. ISRO's Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV) is commonly used for deploying Earth Observation Satellites.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. Earth observation satellites are indeed used for weather forecasting and climate monitoring (Statement 1). Hyperspectral imaging provides detailed spectral information for material analysis (Statement 2). PSLV is a workhorse launch vehicle for ISRO, often used for deploying Earth observation satellites into polar sun-synchronous orbits (Statement 3).

prelimsMocks.question: With respect to the Electoral Bonds Scheme, which of the following statements is NOT correct?
options: (a) Electoral bonds are issued by the State Bank of India (SBI).
(b) The bonds are valid for a period of 15 days from the date of issue.
(c) Only political parties registered under Section 29A of RPA and securing at least 1% votes in the last election are eligible to receive bonds.
(d) Donor details are publicly disclosed by the issuing bank.
prelimsMocks.answer: (d) Donor details are publicly disclosed by the issuing bank.
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement (d) is NOT correct. A key feature of the Electoral Bonds Scheme is the anonymity of donors. The issuing bank (SBI) does not disclose donor details to the public, although this aspect has been a subject of debate and legal challenges. Statements (a), (b), and (c) are correct features of the scheme.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a member country of ASEAN?
prelimsMocks.explanation: South Korea is not a member of ASEAN. The ten member countries of ASEAN are Brunei Darussalam, Cambodia, Indonesia, Lao PDR, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam.

mainsMocks.question: Analyze the strategic imperative behind India's National Green Hydrogen Mission and the proposed Green Hydrogen Corridor. Discuss the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and suggest measures to overcome them. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy; Environment Conservation & Pollution Control; Science and Technology-developments & their applications & effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce NGHM and Green Hydrogen Corridor's goals (energy security, climate targets). Discuss challenges like high production costs, technology gaps, infrastructure development (storage, transport), and regulatory hurdles. Suggest solutions: R&D investment, policy incentives, international collaboration, market creation, and grid integration strategies.

mainsMocks.question: Critically examine the impact of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 on transforming India's education landscape. What are the key implementation challenges, and how can they be effectively addressed? (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by outlining NEP 2020's vision (holistic, multidisciplinary, skill-based). Discuss positive impacts: curriculum reform, early childhood care, vocational education. Identify challenges: teacher training, infrastructure, digital divide, funding, state-center coordination, resistance to change. Suggest solutions: increased budget, phased implementation, robust teacher development, public-private partnerships, technology integration, community involvement.

mainsMocks.question: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) has been a testament to enduring water diplomacy between India and Pakistan. In light of increasing climate change impacts and water scarcity, discuss the evolving challenges to the IWT and the potential for enhanced cooperation between the riparian states. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: India and its neighborhood- relations; Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests; Important Geophysical phenomena.
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly describe IWT's importance. Explain how climate change (glacier melt, erratic rainfall) impacts water flows, creating new stresses. Discuss challenges: declining water availability, differing interpretations, need for adaptation. Suggest cooperation: joint research on climate impacts, data sharing, flood forecasting, exploring new storage solutions, and strengthening the Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) mechanism.

quickBytes.text: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission aims for 5 million tonnes production by 2030.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) sets India's benchmark interest rates.
ISRO's PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is a key launch vehicle for Earth Observation Satellites.
Electoral Bonds were introduced in India in 2017 to facilitate political funding.
ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) has 10 member states in Southeast Asia.
Project Tiger was launched in India in 1973 for tiger conservation.
NEP 2020 proposes a new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure for school education.
The 'Polluter Pays Principle' holds polluters responsible for environmental damage costs.
India aims for 500 GW non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030, pledged at COP26.
The Indus Waters Treaty (1960) allocates Western Rivers to Pakistan and Eastern Rivers to India.

GS1.headline: Ancient Buddhist Site in Ladakh Proposed for UNESCO World Heritage Status
GS1.explainer:
• The Indian government has formally proposed an ancient Buddhist monastery complex in Ladakh for inclusion in the UNESCO World Heritage List.
• The site, known for its unique monastic architecture, pristine murals, and invaluable Buddhist scriptures, represents a significant cultural and historical legacy.
• This nomination aims to protect and preserve the fragile heritage of the Himalayan region and promote cultural tourism.
• UNESCO experts are expected to conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the site's outstanding universal value.
• Inclusion would bring international recognition and support for conservation efforts.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: UNESCO World Heritage Sites are places of outstanding universal value to humanity.
Ladakh is known for its unique Tibetan Buddhist culture.
ASI is the premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India.
India has 42 UNESCO World Heritage Sites (as of 2023 end).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Buddhist heritage sites in India for understanding its cultural and historical linkages with other Asian countries.
Analyze the challenges in preserving ancient monastic complexes in geographically remote and climatically harsh regions like Ladakh.
Evaluate the benefits and responsibilities associated with a site being designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Examine the role of cultural tourism in promoting local economies while ensuring sustainable heritage conservation.
GS1.imageDescription: An ancient Buddhist monastery nestled amidst the mountains of Ladakh.
GS1.trivia: The Hemis Monastery in Ladakh is one of the largest and wealthiest monasteries in the region.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Standing Committee Recommends Reforms for Electoral Bonds Scheme
GS2.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee on Law and Justice today tabled its report recommending significant reforms to the existing Electoral Bonds Scheme.
• The committee emphasized the need for greater transparency and accountability in political funding to strengthen democratic processes.
• Key recommendations include lowering the minimum denomination of bonds and introducing a mechanism for public disclosure of donor details.
• The report also suggested exploring alternative methods of political financing that are more equitable and transparent.
• This comes amidst ongoing debates and public scrutiny regarding the opaque nature of the current electoral bonds system.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Accountability and transparency in governance
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bonds scheme introduced in 2017 via Finance Act.
Allows citizens/corporate bodies to donate to political parties anonymously.
Bonds issued by State Bank of India (SBI) in specific denominations.
Valid for 15 days from the date of issue.
Only political parties registered under Section 29A of RPA and securing at least 1% votes in last general/assembly election are eligible to receive bonds.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the arguments for and against the Electoral Bonds Scheme.
Discuss how lack of transparency in political funding impacts democratic accountability and fairness of elections.
Examine the various recommendations for electoral reforms concerning political financing in India.
Evaluate the role of parliamentary committees in scrutinizing government policies and suggesting improvements.
GS2.imageDescription: Image of the Parliament building in New Delhi.
GS2.trivia: The Supreme Court is currently hearing petitions challenging the constitutionality of the Electoral Bonds Scheme.

GS2.headline: India-ASEAN Summit Concludes with Focus on Maritime Security and Trade Enhancement
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Summit concluded today, with leaders emphasizing deeper cooperation in maritime security, trade, and digital connectivity.
• Discussions focused on countering non-traditional security threats in the Indo-Pacific, including piracy, terrorism, and illegal fishing.
• A new roadmap for enhancing bilateral trade to $300 billion by 2030 was adopted, alongside initiatives for supply chain resilience.
• India reiterated its commitment to ASEAN centrality in its 'Act East' policy and the broader Indo-Pacific vision.
• Agreements were also signed on cultural exchange programs and capacity building in emerging technologies.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
keyTerms: SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region)
prelimsPoints: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) founded in 1967.
Members: Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Brunei, Vietnam, Laos, Myanmar, Cambodia.
India became a Sectoral Dialogue Partner of ASEAN in 1992, Dialogue Partner in 1995, and Summit-level partner in 2002.
India's 'Act East Policy' is a cornerstone of its engagement with ASEAN countries.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of India's 'Act East Policy' in the context of its strategic engagement with ASEAN.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for enhancing maritime security cooperation between India and ASEAN in the Indo-Pacific region.
Evaluate the potential for increasing bilateral trade and investment between India and ASEAN, identifying key sectors for growth.
Examine how India's role in ASEAN contributes to regional stability and economic integration.
GS2.imageDescription: Leaders from India and ASEAN member states at a summit meeting.
GS2.trivia: The theme of this year's summit revolved around 'Strengthening Partnership for a Sustainable and Resilient Future'.

GS2.headline: NEP 2020: Report Highlights Implementation Challenges and Path Forward
GS2.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by a government-appointed task force today highlighted both successes and persistent challenges in the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• While significant progress has been made in curriculum reforms and digital learning initiatives, issues like teacher training and infrastructure gaps remain.
• The report emphasized the need for increased public spending on education and greater collaboration between central and state governments.
• Recommendations include targeted capacity building programs for educators and phased implementation strategies for complex reforms.
• The policy aims to transform India's education system from school to higher education over the next decade.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
Aims for 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in preschool to secondary level by 2030.
Proposes 5+3+3+4 curricular structure.
Focus on multidisciplinary education, vocational training, and critical thinking.
Recommends increasing public investment in education to 6% of GDP.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of NEP 2020 in addressing the shortcomings of India's education system.
Discuss the major challenges in implementing NEP 2020, particularly concerning teacher readiness, infrastructure, and funding.
Suggest concrete measures to overcome the implementation hurdles and ensure equitable access to quality education.
Evaluate the role of technology and digital platforms in achieving the objectives of NEP 2020.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing educational advancement.
GS2.trivia: The NEP 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India.

GS2.headline: High Court Directs State to Implement Comprehensive Urban Waste Management Plan
GS2.explainer:
• The State High Court today issued a landmark directive ordering the state government to formulate and implement a comprehensive urban waste management plan within six months.
• The ruling came in response to a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) highlighting severe environmental degradation and public health hazards caused by unscientific waste disposal.
• The court emphasized the 'polluter pays' principle and the need for decentralized waste processing units.
• It also mandated strict enforcement of solid waste management rules and public awareness campaigns.
• This judgment sets a precedent for other states grappling with similar waste management challenges.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Judiciary
prelimsPoints: Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016, replaced the Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000.
PIL allows public-spirited individuals or organizations to approach courts for public good.
Polluter Pays Principle states that those who produce pollution should bear the costs of managing it.
Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) are primarily responsible for waste management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-faceted challenges faced by Indian cities in effective urban waste management.
Analyze the role of judicial activism, particularly through PILs, in compelling government action on environmental issues.
Suggest innovative solutions and best practices for sustainable solid waste management in urban areas.
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing environmental laws and policies in addressing waste pollution.
GS2.imageDescription: An illustration of proper waste segregation and recycling bins in an urban setting.
GS2.trivia: India generates approximately 1.5 lakh metric tonnes of municipal solid waste per day.

GS2.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Enter Critical Phase
GS2.explainer:
• Negotiations for the India-European Union Free Trade Agreement (FTA) have entered a critical and final phase, with both sides expressing optimism for a breakthrough.
• Senior negotiators met today to resolve outstanding issues related to tariffs, intellectual property rights, and market access for services.
• A successful FTA is expected to significantly boost bilateral trade and investment, creating new opportunities for businesses and consumers.
• Key areas of contention include agricultural subsidies and data localization norms, requiring careful diplomatic efforts.
• The agreement aims to unlock the full potential of the India-EU strategic partnership.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: European Union (EU) is a political and economic union of 27 member states.
India-EU relations elevated to a 'Strategic Partnership' in 2007.
FTA negotiations began in 2007, paused in 2013, and resumed in 2022.
The EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential benefits and challenges of a comprehensive Free Trade Agreement between India and the European Union.
Discuss the key contentious issues in the India-EU FTA negotiations and their implications for India's domestic industries.
Evaluate how a successful FTA with the EU could strengthen India's position in global trade and supply chains.
Examine the broader geopolitical significance of enhanced India-EU economic cooperation.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union waving side-by-side.
GS2.trivia: The EU is India's third-largest trading partner, accounting for over 10% of India's total trade.

GS2.headline: Digital India Initiative Expands with New Unified Citizen Services Portal
GS2.explainer:
• Building on the success of the Digital India program, the government today launched a new unified citizen services portal, integrating multiple government services under a single digital platform.
• The portal aims to enhance ease of access to public services, reduce red tape, and improve transparency and efficiency in governance.
• Citizens can now access services ranging from birth certificates and land records to utility bill payments and grievance redressal through one interface.
• This initiative leverages advanced technologies like AI-powered chatbots and blockchain for secure data management.
• The expansion is expected to further bridge the digital divide and empower citizens with seamless access to government services.
gsSubTopics: E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: Digital India program launched in 2015.
Aims to transform India into a digitally empowered society and knowledge economy.
Key pillars include Digital Infrastructure, Governance & Services on Demand, and Digital Empowerment of Citizens.
E-governance focuses on using ICT to improve government services.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and impact of the Digital India program in enhancing public service delivery and citizen engagement.
Analyze the benefits of a unified citizen services portal in improving governance, transparency, and reducing corruption.
Examine the challenges associated with digital inclusion, data security, and ensuring equitable access to e-governance services.
Evaluate how technology can be leveraged to further streamline government processes and foster participatory governance.
GS2.imageDescription: A person using a smartphone to access online government services.
GS2.trivia: India's JAM (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile) trinity has been instrumental in direct benefit transfers and financial inclusion.

GS2.headline: G20 Finance Ministers Meet to Discuss Global Economic Cooperation and Debt Relief
GS2.explainer:
• Finance Ministers and Central Bank Governors from G20 nations convened today to address pressing global economic challenges, including inflation, growth slowdown, and debt vulnerabilities.
• Discussions focused on coordinating macroeconomic policies, strengthening financial stability, and promoting sustainable development.
• A key agenda item was the implementation of debt relief initiatives for low-income countries and reforms to multilateral development banks.
• India, as a prominent G20 member, reiterated its call for equitable global growth and climate financing for developing nations.
• The meeting aimed to build consensus on strategies to foster a resilient and inclusive global economy.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: G20 (Group of Twenty) is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
It works to address major issues related to the global economy.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023.
Common Framework for Debt Treatments beyond the DSSI (Debt Service Suspension Initiative) is a G20 initiative.
mainsPoints: Analyze the role of the G20 in fostering global economic stability and addressing contemporary challenges like inflation and debt crises.
Discuss India's priorities and contributions to the G20 agenda, particularly concerning climate finance and equitable growth.
Examine the effectiveness of multilateral platforms like the G20 in coordinating international responses to economic downturns.
Evaluate the need for reforms in multilateral development banks to better serve the needs of developing countries.
GS2.imageDescription: G20 logo prominently displayed at an international conference.
GS2.trivia: The G20 represents around two-thirds of the world's population, 85% of global GDP, and 75% of global trade.

GS2.headline: India-Pakistan Hold Dialogue on Indus Waters Treaty Review Amidst Climate Concerns
GS2.explainer:
• Officials from India and Pakistan held bilateral talks today to review aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), focusing on current water-sharing arrangements and climate change impacts.
• The dialogue comes amidst increasing concerns over water scarcity and the changing hydrological patterns in the Indus basin due to global warming.
• Both sides discussed proposals for technical adjustments to the treaty and mechanisms for dispute resolution.
• While the IWT has largely withstood conflicts, climate change presents new challenges requiring cooperative solutions.
• The talks aim to ensure equitable and sustainable water management for both riparian states.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) signed in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
Allocates Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral commission for implementation of the treaty.
The treaty is considered one of the most successful water-sharing agreements globally.
mainsPoints: Analyze the enduring significance of the Indus Waters Treaty as a framework for water sharing between India and Pakistan.
Discuss how climate change and increasing water scarcity are challenging the existing provisions of the IWT.
Examine the potential for India and Pakistan to enhance cooperation on transboundary water management and disaster preparedness.
Evaluate the role of water diplomacy in mitigating tensions and fostering regional stability in South Asia.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River basin and its tributaries flowing through India and Pakistan.
GS2.trivia: The Indus River system is one of the largest river systems in the world, originating in the Tibetan Plateau.

GS2.headline: Gender Budgeting Report Highlights Progress and Gaps in Women-Centric Schemes
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Finance today released its annual Gender Budgeting Statement, reporting on the allocation and expenditure for women-centric schemes and programs.
• The report highlighted increased allocation in key sectors like education, health, and skill development, showing progress towards gender equality.
• However, it also identified implementation gaps and the need for more targeted interventions to address specific challenges faced by women.
• Recommendations include strengthening monitoring mechanisms and ensuring gender-disaggregated data collection for better policy formulation.
• Gender budgeting is a tool to analyze the government's budget from a gender perspective, ensuring equitable resource distribution.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Gender Budgeting is not a separate budget but an analysis of the government budget from a gender perspective.
It was introduced in India in 2005-06.
It comprises two parts: Part A (100% women-specific schemes) and Part B (schemes with at least 30% allocation for women).
SDG 5 aims to achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept and objectives of gender budgeting in India, and its role in promoting women's empowerment.
Analyze the progress and persistent challenges highlighted in the latest Gender Budgeting Statement regarding women-centric schemes.
Examine the importance of gender-disaggregated data and robust monitoring mechanisms for effective policy implementation.
Evaluate the impact of government schemes on various aspects of women's lives, including economic, social, and political empowerment.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic showing gender budgeting allocations across different sectors.
GS2.trivia: India was one of the first countries in Asia to adopt gender budgeting.

GS3.headline: India Unveils Ambitious 'National Green Hydrogen Corridor' Project
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government today launched the 'National Green Hydrogen Corridor' project, a major step towards achieving India's ambitious climate targets and energy independence.
• The project aims to establish dedicated infrastructure for the production, storage, and transportation of green hydrogen across key industrial hubs.
• It includes setting up large-scale electrolyser manufacturing facilities, pipelines for hydrogen transport, and storage depots.
• The initiative is a cornerstone of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission, targeting a production capacity of 5 million tonnes per annum by 2030.
• This move is expected to attract significant domestic and foreign investment, creating numerous job opportunities in the green energy sector.
gsSubTopics: Energy
Infrastructure
Environment Conservation & Pollution Control
Science & Technology-developments & their applications & effects in everyday life
Economic Development
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) launched in 2023.
Target: 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen production by 2030.
Corridor aims to connect production sites to consumption centres.
Focus on reducing fossil fuel imports and achieving net-zero emissions by 2070.
Scheme components include incentives for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategic rationale behind prioritizing green hydrogen for energy security and climate goals.
Analyze the economic implications of the Green Hydrogen Corridor, including job creation, investment, and export potential.
Examine the technological challenges and opportunities in scaling up green hydrogen production and infrastructure.
Evaluate the role of government policies and international collaborations in realizing the vision of a hydrogen economy.
Critically assess the environmental benefits and potential drawbacks of large-scale green hydrogen deployment.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration showing a map of India with proposed Green Hydrogen Corridor routes and production facilities.
GS3.trivia: India's target of 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen by 2030 is equivalent to displacing about 50 million tonnes of fossil fuels.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Inflationary Concerns
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) today decided to maintain the repo rate at its current level for the seventh consecutive time.
• This decision comes amidst persistent global inflationary pressures and the need to support domestic economic growth.
• RBI Governor highlighted the robust growth trajectory of the Indian economy but cautioned about potential headwinds from global commodity prices.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting growth.
• Experts anticipate continued vigilance from the RBI, with future policy moves contingent on evolving inflation and growth dynamics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
prelimsPoints: Repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
MPC is a six-member body responsible for setting benchmark interest rates.
Primary objective of monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Inflation target for RBI is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth.
Discuss the impact of a 'status quo' monetary policy on various sectors of the Indian economy.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the MPC framework in achieving its stated objectives.
Examine the role of global economic factors in shaping India's domestic monetary policy decisions.
GS3.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) was constituted in 2016.

GS3.headline: ISRO's 'Shakti' Mission Successfully Deploys Advanced Earth Observation Satellite
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched the 'Shakti' mission, deploying an advanced Earth observation satellite into orbit.
• The satellite is equipped with cutting-edge hyperspectral imaging capabilities, designed for detailed monitoring of natural resources and environmental changes.
• Data from 'Shakti' will be crucial for agricultural planning, disaster management, urban development, and climate studies.
• This mission reinforces India's growing prowess in space technology and its commitment to utilizing space for societal benefit.
• The satellite has a planned operational life of five years, providing continuous high-resolution data.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Space Technology
Disaster Management
prelimsPoints: ISRO is India's national space agency.
Hyperspectral imaging allows for detailed analysis of materials on Earth's surface.
Earth observation satellites are vital for applications like weather forecasting, climate monitoring, and resource mapping.
PSLV and GSLV are ISRO's primary launch vehicles.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Earth observation satellites for India's development and security.
Analyze how hyperspectral imaging technology can contribute to sustainable agriculture and environmental monitoring.
Examine the challenges and future prospects for India's space program in the context of global competition.
Evaluate the socio-economic benefits derived from ISRO's various satellite missions.
GS3.imageDescription: Artist's impression of the 'Shakti' Earth observation satellite in orbit.
GS3.trivia: India is one of the few countries with indigenous capability to launch satellites and develop advanced remote sensing technologies.

GS3.headline: New Tiger Census Shows Promising Recovery in Western Ghats Landscape
GS3.explainer:
• The latest All India Tiger Estimation (AITE) report indicates a significant recovery in tiger populations across the Western Ghats landscape.
• Conservation efforts, including habitat improvement, anti-poaching measures, and community involvement, are credited for the positive trend.
• The report highlights specific areas within the Western Ghats, such as the Periyar and Anamalai Tiger Reserves, showing robust growth.
• This news underscores the success of India's Project Tiger and its commitment to biodiversity conservation.
• Challenges remain in ensuring habitat connectivity and mitigating human-wildlife conflict in the region.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity
Wildlife Protection
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: All India Tiger Estimation (AITE) is conducted every four years.
India is home to over 70% of the world's tiger population.
Project Tiger launched in 1973.
Western Ghats is one of the 36 global biodiversity hotspots.
NTCA is the statutory body under MoEFCC for tiger conservation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the factors contributing to the success of tiger conservation in India, with specific reference to the Western Ghats.
Analyze the socio-economic implications of tiger conservation for local communities and indigenous populations.
Examine the challenges in maintaining habitat connectivity and managing human-wildlife conflict in densely populated areas.
Evaluate the role of international cooperation and technological advancements (e.g., M-STrIPES) in wildlife protection.
GS3.imageDescription: A majestic Bengal Tiger captured in its natural habitat in the Western Ghats.
GS3.trivia: The Western Ghats is older than the Himalayas and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

GS3.headline: Major Boost to 'Make in India' in Defense: New Drone Manufacturing Facility Inaugurated
GS3.explainer:
• In a significant push for indigenous defense manufacturing, a state-of-the-art Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) manufacturing facility was inaugurated in Bengaluru today.
